items.description: Public struct/class `Greeter` with method `greet(name: String) -> String` returning "Hello, <name>!" and `greet() -> String` returning "Hello, world!"

rules_checked.reasoning: Declared kind is 'type' and the item defines a concrete Swift struct/class with two methods — this is exactly a type declaration, not a protocol, extension, or free function. Kind matches what is actually being built.

Target is Sources/Greeter/Greeter.swift, inside the Greeter target defined in Package.swift (.target(name: "Greeter")). This is the sole module in the package and the natural home for the Greeter type; the acceptance criteria also require @testable import Greeter to see it, confirming the module is correct.

Greeter is a plain value/service type producing greeting strings with no UI or persistence involved — there are no views or view models in this package, so no altitude violation (no domain logic in views, no data access from view models) is possible or present here.

This is the first substantive type in a fresh scaffold module (only GreeterModule.swift, an empty namespace marker, exists so far). A new file for the new type is warranted rather than shoehorning it into the placeholder marker enum; granularity matches the single well-defined responsibility (producing greetings).
The item introduces a concrete struct/class only — no protocol is proposed. No speculative abstraction is being introduced, so this rule is satisfied trivially (nothing to flag).

`Greeter` is a noun naming a thing that greets, following Swift API Design Guidelines for type names. `greet(name:)` and `greet()` are verb-named methods matching Swift's guideline that mutating/action methods use base verb form; parameter label `name` is clear and unabbreviated.

The type is self-contained within Sources/Greeter with no described imports of other modules or forbidden dependencies; Package.swift shows Greeter has no external dependencies, so no dependency-direction or forbidden-import issue arises.
